current.summary: Deep research on ACP trust anchor design; ready to run multi-agent panel discussion on 5 open questions.

activeWork.description: Integrating the trust anchor mechanism (acp-trust-anchor.md) into the ACP spec (acp-spec.md). Research phase complete. Panel discussion phase next.
activeWork.progress: 1) Walked through 7 brainstorming questions with Holden. 2 decided immediately (test vectors: yes; SSH: acknowledge gap + optional platform key discovery). 4 sent to panel (#3 platform-as-publisher, #4 source_repo dual role, #5 host trust, #6 rollback). #7 intro rewrite deferred until after panel. 2) Ran 4 deep research agents in parallel (OIDC formats, SSH APIs, Sigstore federation, supply chain trust models). 3) Research revealed 3 critical findings that change the design. 4) Research synthesis saved to docs/spec/acp/acp-trust-anchor-research.md. 5) Panel discussion needs to be architected as 5 independent agents with a message bus — NOT a single agent playing all roles.

recentDecisions: Test vectors: YES — add TVs for OIDC subject extraction per provider (GitHub, GitLab). Prevents implementation divergence on the most security-critical step.

SSH gap: Both A+D — be honest about SSH not having source binding, but define optional platform key discovery (GitHub /users/{username}/ssh_signing_keys) as an OPTIONAL verification step. SSH stays valid but reported as 'not source-bound' without it.

Extraction algorithm must change: Do NOT parse the `sub` claim. Extract Source Repository URI from Fulcio certificate OID extension 1.3.6.1.4.1.57264.1.12. Strip https:// and compare to source_repo with exact string equality. Simpler, more robust, handles GitLab subgroups.
Forgejo cannot sign today: Remove or correct Forgejo from provider tables in both the spec (Appendix C) and trust anchor doc.
Intro rewrite: Write AFTER panel resolves design questions, not before.
nextSteps: Architect the multi-agent panel: 5 independent agents (Platform Vendor, Enterprise Security, Solo Publisher, Registry Operator, Spec Purist) with a shared message bus file (JSONL). Each agent reads the bus, writes its response. Coordinator appends responses between rounds. 5 rounds max.

Run the panel on 5 revised questions: (1) Platform-as-publisher + source_repo dual role, (2) Host trust + Sigstore instance trust, (3) Version rollback + trust anchor, (4) Bootstrap problem (first-publish), (5) sigstore-a2a alignment.
After panel: synthesize consensus, update acp-trust-anchor.md with resolved positions, then integrate into acp-spec.md.

context.notes:
CRITICAL ARCHITECTURE DECISION: The panel must use 5 separate agents, not 1 agent playing 5 roles. Single-agent multi-persona will self-rationalize and converge artificially. The message bus pattern: a shared JSONL file where each agent appends statements, reads others' statements before responding. This creates genuine independent reasoning with cross-agent interaction.

The research file (docs/spec/acp/acp-trust-anchor-research.md) contains the full synthesis of all 4 research tracks and should be the primary context document for the panel agents.

Key prior art to reference: npm provenance is the closest analogue to ACP's source_repo binding. PyPI Trusted Publishers have the strongest authorization model (explicit pre-registration). sigstore-a2a is directly relevant as it signs AI Agent Cards.

Holden's framing: He's not trying to solve individual identity — he's writing the spec that tells publishers HOW to build the trust chain. ACP is the interoperability contract that platforms implement. The trust anchor doc is the missing piece that makes the chain meaningful.
